retrieved_docs.title: Urinalysis -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings -- Glucose
retrieved_docs.content: Glycosuria occurs when the filtered load of glucose exceeds the tubular cells' ability to reabsorb it, which normally happens at a glucose serum concentration of around 180  mg per dL. Furthermore, nitrites are not normally found in urine, and it is highly specific for urinary tract infection. However, due to its low sensitivity, a negative result does not rule out infection. [14] Normal: Negative Associations: Diabetes mellitus, Cushing syndrome, Fanconi syndrome, glucose infusion, pregnancy. Glucosuria with normal plasma glucose without other features of Fanconi syndrome is due to a benign condition referred to as renal glycosuria and is due to a mutation in the sodium-glucose linked transporter 2 False-positive: Ketones, levodopa False-negative: Elevated specific gravity, uric acid, vitamin C [7] [11] [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glycosuria -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: One limitation of urine glucose testing is the ability of most commercial semiquantitative urine tests to detect glucose in the urine only until it reaches a level of 50 to 250 mg/dl. Also, errors can stem from an altered renal threshold. There are known variations in the renal threshold among individuals that can lead to significantly misleading data.

retrieved_docs.title: Renal Function Tests -- Procedures -- Urine Analysis
retrieved_docs.content: In healthy urine specimens, urine protein is negative. Bilirubin is not detected in normal urine. Glucose is not detected in healthy patients but may be observed in diabetes mellitus, pregnancy, and renal glycosuria. Ascorbic acid (vitamin C) supplements may cause false-negative results for hemoglobin, leukocyte esterase, nitrite, and glucose. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glycosuria -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Glycosuria is a term that defines the presence of reducing sugars in the urine, such as glucose, galactose, lactose, fructose, etc. Glucosuria connotes the presence of glucose in the urine and is the most frequent type of glycosuria, which is the focus of this review. It happens when the glomerulus filters more glucose than the proximal tubule can reabsorb. In normal individuals, glucosuria can be up to 0.25 mg/ml. More than 0.25 mg/ml in random fresh urine is considered increased glucosuria and can be due to elevated plasma glucose, renal glucose absorption impairment, or both. [1] [2] Physiologic glucosuria is a condition where individuals consume an excessive amount of carbohydrates.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glycosuria -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Small amounts of glucose in the urine are considered normal, but glucosuria usually refers to pathologic conditions where the amounts of urine glucose are more than 25 mg/dl in random fresh urine. The renal tubule normally reabsorbs almost all (leaving less than 25 mg/dl urine glucose) in the normal glomerular filtrate. When the glucose filtrated by the glomerular exceeds the capacity of the renal tubule to absorb it, the loss of balance occurs. It can happen due to elevated plasma glucose, as in diabetes mellitus, or when the ability of the tubule to absorb glucose is impaired, eg, Fanconi syndrome with impairment in the absorption of phosphate, amino acids, or isolated glucosuria as an inherited disorder termed Familial Renal glucosuria.

retrieved_docs.title: Plasma Glucose -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: The diagnosis of DM has serious consequences. DM confers a risk of long-term diabetic complications, including blindness, renal failure, and amputations, as well as an increased risk of cardiovascular disease. The condition also entails a lifetime of dietary restrictions and medications and can seriously curtail lifestyle and employment prospects. [37] The diagnosis may be suggested by the patient’s history or by the results of dipstick tests for glucose on urine specimens. However, urine glucose measurements are inadequate for diagnosing DM. [63] They potentially yield false-positive results in subjects with a low renal threshold for glucose. A patient with DM may deliver false-negative results if the patient is fasting. [64]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glycosuria -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: A study comparing the urine and plasma response 60 minutes after a 50 g oral glucose challenge for patients with potential gestational diabetes mellitus screening showed that post-load glycosuria is a poor predictor of gestational diabetes mellitus, pre-eclampsia, and newborn size at birth. It has a limited clinical benefit. [9] In diabetes mellitus type 2, there appears to be a maladaptive upregulation of SGLT2 that contributes to hyperglycemia. A significant advance in diabetes treatment is the ushering in of the SGLT2 inhibitor class of drugs. These drugs have been demonstrated to improve glycemic control with weight loss by inducing glycosuria with calorie loss. They also promote natriuresis, which results in a decrease in blood pressure. However, they can increase the risk of genital and urinary tract infections and dehydration. [10]

retrieved_docs.content: Urine is positive for the reducing sugars test but negative for the glucose dipstick test. An aldolase B enzyme assay in a liver biopsy and DNA profiling by allele-specific probes serve as confirmatory diagnostic tests. In addition, monitoring increased carbohydrate-deficient transferrin by transferrin isoelectric focusing and increased activity of aspartylglucosaminidase should be used for disease follow-up to assess compliance with dietary restrictions.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Glycosuria -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Glucosuria can also occur in increased glucose concentration in the circulating blood. This phenomenon can also occur in normal individuals who consume excess carbohydrates, known as ‘alimentary glycosuria.’ It also presents in diabetic patients. [8] In diabetes mellitus, glomeruli can be damaged with increasing duration, resulting in albuminuria and a decrease in the glomerular filtration rate. In diabetic patients, the kidneys are more susceptible to the effects of hyperglycemia; many of the kidney cells cannot decrease glucose transport rates and prevent intercellular hyperglycemia in an increased glucose concentration state. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Blood Glucose Monitoring -- Clinical Significance -- Hyperglycemia
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms of hyperglycemia include polyuria (increased and frequent urination), polydipsia (increased thirst), blurred vision, headache, fatigue, and glucosuria. Acute symptoms of hyperglycemia are not usually seen at levels below 14 mmol/L or 250 mg/dL.

retrieved_docs.title: Plasma Glucose -- Specimen Requirements and Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Fluoride ions in high concentration inhibit the activity of urease and certain other enzymes; consequently, the specimens are unsuitable for determining urea in procedures requiring urease and for the direct assay of some serum enzymes. [46] Potassium oxalate (K 2 C 2 O 4 ) causes cell water loss, diluting the plasma. Therefore, samples collected in these tubes should not be used to measure analytes other than glucose. [47] Although fluoride maintains long-term blood glucose stability, the rate of decline in the first hour after sample collection is not altered, and glycolysis may continue for up to 4 hours. [48]

retrieved_docs.title: Hexokinase Method -- Specimen Requirements and Procedure
retrieved_docs.content: Glucose concentration in 24-hour urine specimens is scarcely prescribed. Variation in glucose concentration is very high in such urine specimens due to bacterial activity, temperature, and pH-related effects. Commercially available diagnostic kits are designed to estimate glucose levels in plasma or serum specimens. Urine glucose levels in a person with uncontrolled diabetes mellitus are too high for these kits’ linearity. If needed to be processed, urine samples should be adjusted for pH, linearity range, and other interfering substances utilizing NAD+/NADP+ or absorbance at 340 nm. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Blood Glucose Monitoring -- Clinical Significance -- Hyperglycemia
retrieved_docs.content: Untreated hyperglycemia may also result in a hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state. This is a rare condition seen most commonly in clients with type 2 diabetes mellitus. Profound hyperglycemia can result in significant glucosuria; because glucose is hydrophilic, the kidney produces exceedingly large volumes of urine, resulting in life-threatening dehydration and potential coma. Both diabetic ketoacidosis and hyperosmolar state require emergent management to reduce elevated blood glucose levels with insulin therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperglycemia -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: When evaluating a patient for hyperglycemia, the focus should be on the patient's cardiorespiratory status, mental status, and volume status. Bedside serum glucose can be obtained quickly. Testing includes serum electrolytes with the calculation of the anion gap, blood urea nitrogen and creatinine, and complete blood count. Urinalysis by dipstick assesses glucose and ketones in the urine. Arterial blood gas or venous blood gas may be necessary if serum bicarbonate is substantially reduced. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Urinalysis -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Urinalysis is an ancient diagnostic screening test that has stood the test of time and is still useful in clinical laboratories since it plays a critical role in the health assessment process. [11] [13] For some, a urinalysis is considered as the most common, simple, and relevant screening exam that provides clinicians with valuable information about the general health status of a patient, including hydration, urinary tract infection, diabetes mellitus, and liver or renal disease. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality (ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما) is clinically associated with high blood glucose (ارتفاع السكر في الدم), indicating that hyperglycemia often contributes to or results from elevated osmotic concentration in the blood. This condition is also linked to urinary tract disorders (اضطراب السبيل البولي), suggesting that disturbances of the urinary system can be an underlying or related factor in the development of high plasma osmolality. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the close interplay between osmotic balance, fluid volume status, and glucose regulation. Recognizing that hyperglycemia, urinary tract pathology, and hypovolemia commonly intersect with elevated plasma osmolality helps clinicians assess underlying causes and guide comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1919
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism include the SMQ titled “ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد” (new‑onset diabetes mellitus with elevated blood glucose), and this SMQ in turn comprises the clinical effect “تأثير إدرار البول” (increased urine output). This relationship indicates that new‑onset diabetes, classified within lipid metabolism disorders, is associated with the manifestation of polyuria, highlighting the interconnected nature of metabolic dysregulation and its renal‑related symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1625
retrieved_docs.content: ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما is recognized as a clinical manifestation that is closely linked to ارتفاع السكر في الدم and حالة نفسية غير طبيعية. It is considered a subcategory of اضطراب السبيل البولي, indicating that disturbances of the urinary tract can give rise to or be associated with elevated plasma osmolality. The condition is also mapped from التجفاف, reflecting that dehydration is a primary physiological driver of increased plasma osmolality. In addition, ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما frequently co‑occurs مع قلة حجم الدم, highlighting the common association between high plasma osmolality and reduced blood volume. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of evaluating hydration status, blood glucose levels, mental status, and urinary tract function when assessing patients with elevated plasma osmolality.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1974
retrieved_docs.content: ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) is closely linked with التجفاف and تأثير إدرار البول. The condition of new‑onset diabetes, characterized by elevated blood glucose, commonly produces an osmotic diuresis (تأثير إدرار البول), which increases urine output and can lead to dehydration (التجفاف). Recognizing that التجفاف is a manifestation of the SMQ and that تأثير إدرار البول is a constituent feature helps clinicians monitor fluid balance and manage patients with new‑onset diabetes more effectively.

The urinary tract disorder may raise plasma osmolality, which in turn is associated with dehydration, low blood volume, insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes, and altered mental state. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2157
retrieved_docs.content: Increased plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels (hyperglycemia). This rise in plasma osmolality often originates from dehydration, as fluid loss concentrates solutes in the bloodstream. Additionally, increased plasma osmolality commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume (hypovolemia). Recognizing that dehydration can lead to higher plasma osmolality, which in turn may signal hyperglycemia and accompany hypovolemia, is essential for accurate diagnosis and appropriate fluid and glucose management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1687
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين), indicating that patients with this form of diabetes are at heightened risk for sudden deterioration of renal function. In the context of acute kidney failure, abnormalities in extrarenal nitrogenous blood (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية) may develop, reflecting the accumulation of nitrogenous waste products when the kidneys are unable to adequately filter the blood. Moreover, acute kidney failure serves as a parent condition for urinary tract disorders (اضطراب السبيل البولي), suggesting that severe renal impairment can predispose patients to complications of the urinary system. Together, these relationships underscore the importance of vigilant monitoring of renal health in insulin‑dependent type 2 diabetic patients, early detection of nitrogenous waste disturbances, and proactive management of potential urinary tract complications to improve overall patient outcomes.

Hyperglycemia/new‑onset diabetes (ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد) is placed within the dehydration group and includes polyuria (تأثير إدرار البول) as a characteristic feature. Overall, the knowledge base underscores the interdependence of lipid and carbohydrate metabolism, electrolyte and acid‑base homeostasis, endocrine‑pancreatic function, renal health, respiratory and hepatic pathology, and substance‑use effects, highlighting the necessity of integrated assessment and multidisciplinary management for patients presenting with these overlapping conditions.

Hypoglycemia (نقص تركيز السكر) is clinically associated with hyperkalemia, which also appears as a consequence of acute kidney failure and frequently co‑occurs with acidosis. اضطراب إفراز الجسترين is a parent condition that leads to gastric mucosal atrophy and secretion disorders (الحثل واضطرابات الإفراز في الغشاء المخاطي المَعِدِيّ المِعَوِيّ). These relationships illustrate how renal failure, plasma‑osmolality changes, glucose‑concentration abnormalities, electrolyte disturbances, and hormonal disorders are interrelated, emphasizing the need for integrated assessment and management in patients with diabetes, dehydration, and urinary‑tract pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2044
retrieved_docs.content: Elevated plasma osmolality is clinically associated with several forms of diabetes, including type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, and insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes. In addition to its relationship with these diabetic conditions, elevated plasma osmolality frequently co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that reduced circulatory volume may contribute to or exacerbate the increase in plasma osmolality observed in diabetic patients. Recognizing the link between elevated plasma osmolality, various diabetes subtypes, and low blood volume can aid clinicians in monitoring fluid and electrolyte balance, optimizing glycemic management, and preventing complications related to dehydration and hyperosmolar states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1780
retrieved_docs.content: ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما (increased plasma osmolality) is clinically associated with ارتفاع السكر في الدم (hyperglycemia), مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين (insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes), and حالة نفسية غير طبيعية (abnormal mental state). It also co‑occurs with قلة حجم الدم (low blood volume) and can be mapped from التجفاف (dehydration), indicating that dehydration often leads to higher plasma osmolality. التجفاف itself is clinically associated with فشل كلوي حاد (acute kidney failure). فشل كلوي حاد is clinically associated with قلة حجم الدم, مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين, and حالة نفسية غير طبيعية, and it is a subtype of اضطراب السبيل البولي (urinary tract disorder). اضطراب السبيل البولي is the parent category for both فشل كلوي حاد and ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما. These interrelated findings underscore how disturbances in fluid balance, plasma osmolality, and metabolic control can contribute to acute kidney injury and broader urinary system disorders, highlighting the need for careful monitoring of hydration status, blood glucose, and mental state in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 1163
retrieved_docs.content: damage. Elevated plasma osmolality often results from dehydration, is linked to type‑2 diabetes requiring insulin, abnormal mental states, reduced blood volume, and metabolic acidosis accompanied by hyperkalemia; it is classified within urinary tract disorders, illustrating how osmotic, volume‑status, acid‑base, and electrolyte abnormalities intersect. Together, these interrelated concepts highlight the complex interplay among fluid‑electrolyte homeostasis, renal function, metabolic disease, and infectious or inflammatory processes, emphasizing the importance of integrated assessment and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 1264
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is closely linked with type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 2) and its insulin‑requiring form (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين), as well as with dehydration (التجفاف), low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), and abnormal psychological states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية). It is considered a specific manifestation within the broader category of urinary tract disorders (اضطراب السبيل البولي). Increased plasma osmolality (ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما) often arises from dehydration (mapped_from relationship) and co‑occurs with low blood volume. It is also clinically associated with both general type 2 diabetes and the insulin‑requiring subtype, as well as with abnormal psychological states. Increased plasma osmolality is treated as a sub‑entity under urinary tract disorders, indicating that fluid‑electrolyte disturbances are part of the same clinical spectrum. These interconnections illustrate how renal impairment, fluid‑balance abnormalities, metabolic diabetes control, and neuro‑psychological factors overlap, emphasizing the importance of integrated evaluation and coordinated care for patients presenting with any of these related conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2745
retrieved_docs.content: High plasma osmolality, high blood glucose, acute kidney failure, type 2 diabetes, an abnormal psychological state, and low blood volume are closely interrelated clinical entities. High plasma osmolality is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose levels, reflecting the hyperosmolar consequences of uncontrolled diabetes, and it is also linked to type 2 diabetes itself, indicating that patients with this form of diabetes frequently develop increased plasma osmolality. In addition, high plasma osmolality is clinically associated with an abnormal psychological state, consistent with the neurocognitive disturbances that can arise from severe hyperosmolar conditions, and it commonly co‑occurs with low blood volume, suggesting that dehydration or hypovolemia often accompanies the hyperosmolar state. Acute kidney failure is clinically associated with type 2 diabetes, underscoring diabetes as a major risk factor for rapid loss of renal function, and it is also linked to an abnormal psychological state, reflecting the mental status changes that can result from uremic toxins. Moreover, acute kidney failure is clinically associated with low blood volume, indicating that hypovolemia can precipitate or worsen renal injury. Together, these relationships highlight a network in which type 2 diabetes can lead to high plasma osmolality and acute kidney failure, both of which are connected to low blood volume and neuropsychiatric disturbances, emphasizing the importance of monitoring osmolar status, volume balance, renal function, and mental health in patients with diabetes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 725
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure is closely linked with type 2 diabetes, especially insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes, and is frequently observed in patients who are dehydrated, have an abnormal mental state, and present with low blood volume. Dehydration contributes to high plasma osmolality, which in turn is associated with elevated blood glucose and elevated ketone levels in diabetic patients, as well as with abnormal mental status. High plasma osmolality also co‑occurs with reduced blood volume and is considered a manifestation of urinary tract disorder. Elevated ketone levels are clinically associated with the need for foot amputation and with increased extrarenal blood nitrogen (BUN); extrarenal nitrogen is a child finding of acute kidney failure. Both acute kidney failure and high plasma osmolality are children of urinary tract disorder, indicating that urinary tract pathology can precipitate these metabolic and renal complications. The interrelationships among dehydration, high plasma osmolality, ketonemia, acute kidney failure, and type 2 diabetes underscore the importance of monitoring fluid status, renal function, and metabolic control in affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1836
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration (التجفاف) leads to increased plasma osmolality (ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما). Both dehydration and high plasma osmolality are clinically associated with acute kidney injury (فشل كلوي حاد). Acute kidney injury is considered a specific manifestation within urinary tract disorders (اضطراب السبيل البولي), which in turn falls under the broader category of high plasma osmolality. Acute kidney injury is also linked to low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم) and to type 2 diabetes requiring insulin (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين). High plasma osmolality co‑occurs with low blood volume and is clinically associated with the same insulin‑dependent type 2 diabetes as well as with abnormal mental states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية). Abnormal mental states are additionally directly associated with acute kidney injury. Thus, dehydration, elevated plasma osmolality, reduced intravascular volume, insulin‑dependent type 2 diabetes, and neuropsychiatric disturbances are interrelated risk factors and clinical correlates of acute kidney injury within the spectrum of urinary tract disorders.
